connections.name: Virtual Desktop for Vision Pro (Wi-Fi)
requirements: Virtual Desktop purchased on App Store
Virtual Desktop Streamer installed on PC

tracking.controllers: No controllers — hand + eye tracking only (uses VD's virtual Quest controller emulation)

knownIssues.title: No native PCVR runtime — PCVR only via Virtual Desktop
knownIssues.description: Apple does not ship a native PCVR streaming solution. All PC-VR on Vision Pro is currently via Virtual Desktop (third-party, paid). Quest Link / Air Link do not work.
knownIssues.workaround: Purchase Virtual Desktop in the visionOS App Store. Install VD Streamer on your PC. This is the only supported PCVR path.

knownIssues.title: No dedicated controllers — VD emulates Quest Touch via hand tracking
knownIssues.description: Vision Pro has no physical controllers. Virtual Desktop translates hand tracking into virtual Quest Touch controllers. This works well for casual/cinematic VR but poorly for fast motion or precision titles.
knownIssues.workaround: Titles that support hand tracking natively (Eleven Table Tennis, Hand Physics Lab) work best. Precision-heavy titles (Beat Saber, VRChat) feel compromised.

knownIssues.title: 3660×3200 per eye — extreme render demand when not using foveated streaming
knownIssues.description: Virtual Desktop applies foveated encoding by default, but the raw per-eye resolution is among the highest in any consumer VR headset. Without foveated encoding the network bandwidth requirement is impractical.
knownIssues.workaround: Keep VD's foveated encoding ON. Disable only if you have a Wi-Fi 6E 6GHz setup and an NVIDIA 40-series GPU with AV1 encoding.

knownIssues.title: External battery pack limits session length
knownIssues.description: Vision Pro uses an external tethered battery with ~2-2.5h runtime. Long VR sessions require the battery pack connected to power (negating the wireless experience, but charging through USB-C works).
knownIssues.workaround: Keep a USB-C power bank or AC adapter connected to the battery during long sessions.

optimizationTips.tip: Wi-Fi 6E (6GHz) gives the Vision Pro its best wireless VR
optimizationTips.simpleExplanation: Vision Pro supports Wi-Fi 6E. A 6GHz SSID dedicated to the headset provides the bandwidth and interference immunity needed for high-bitrate streaming.
optimizationTips.advancedExplanation: Vision Pro's 6GHz radio handles sustained 200+ Mbps AV1 streams from Virtual Desktop with very low jitter. 5GHz works but caps quality. Dedicate a 6GHz SSID (160 MHz channel) just for the headset and keep other devices on a separate SSID.

optimizationTips.tip: Enable AV1 codec in Virtual Desktop (requires NVIDIA 40/50-series or RX 7000+)
optimizationTips.simpleExplanation: AV1 encoding gives noticeably cleaner image quality than H.265 at the same bitrate on Vision Pro's ultra-high-res panels.
optimizationTips.advancedExplanation: Virtual Desktop Streamer → Video → Codec → AV1 (requires supported GPU). At 150 Mbps AV1 you get comparable quality to ~200 Mbps HEVC, with better motion detail in dark scenes — relevant because Vision Pro's micro-OLED reveals compression artifacts other headsets hide.

optimizationTips.tip: Connect your streaming PC via Ethernet — Wi-Fi + Wi-Fi is unreliable at this resolution
optimizationTips.simpleExplanation: If both your PC and headset are on Wi-Fi, they compete for airtime. With Vision Pro's extreme resolution, this becomes visibly unstable. Use Ethernet on the PC.
optimizationTips.advancedExplanation: PC on Ethernet → router → Vision Pro on 6GHz eliminates the half-duplex Wi-Fi penalty. Measurable -15ms latency improvement in 1080p60 tests on typical consumer hardware.
